posts.name: Stas Medvedev
posts.cooked:
Louis Roederer announced a new collection of still wines Hommage à Camille in Coteaux Champenois appellation.

Two single vineyards cuvees will open the new series:

  • Camille Charmont 2018, 100% Pinot Noir, lieu-dit Charmont, Mareuil-sur-Aÿ;
  • Camille Volibarts 2018, 100% Chardonnay historic vines from lieu-dit Volibarts in Le Mesnil-Sur-Oger

Retail price is set at £150-175

Global warming set a new trend among big houses to expand their portfolios with Coteaux Champenois.

Not long ago Charles Heidsieck launched a limited series of four monocru Coteaux Champenois Blancs: Oger, Villers-Marmery, Montgueux, Vertus.

Let’s see if they succeed in challenging the hegemony of Bollinger with their famous Coteaux Champenois Ay Rouge La Côte aux Enfants.

posts.name: James Kennedy
posts.cooked: I’m fascinated that we’re going to see more and more Coteaux champenois as the region’s climate shifts. When was the tipping point for increased volume of coteaux starting to be produced?

posts.name: Chris Seiber
posts.cooked:
Retail price is set at £150-175

I mean, that’s almost the price of Cristal. Is the demand for these wines that strong?

I’d love to see the world of CC expand, but I’m not really sure why all these wines need to be so expensive, or if they’re so good they justify the prices compared to what you could get from Burgundy, Chablis, California, Oregon, etc.

posts.name: Rodrigo Br@g@
posts.cooked:
Retail price is set at £150-175

I mean, that’s almost the price of Cristal. Is the demand for these wines that strong?

I’d love to see the world of CC expand, but I’m not really sure why all these wines need to be so expensive, or if they’re so good they justify the prices compared to what you could get from Burgundy, Chablis, California, Oregon, etc.

The argument by Champagne producers is that the pricing of Coteaux Champenois is a function of resource allocation and their opportunity cost. The argument goes that each producer only has so much grapes to work with. For every ton of grapes they use to produce Coteaux Champenois, that is a ton less than could use for producing sparkling wine. They could have produced a more “premium” product that could fetch a premium price with those grapes and efforts, but instead produced a still wine, so the price is in part to “recover” some of that lost revenue from foregoing making sparkling with those grapes.

posts.name: William Kelley
posts.cooked:
I’m fascinated that we’re going to see more and more Coteaux champenois as the region’s climate shifts. When was the tipping point for increased volume of coteaux starting to be produced?

Haven’t seen any numbers but I would wager that less Coteaux is made today, as a percentage of the region’s production, than was made in the 1950s!

It is an interesting genre that I think is often held back by unsuitable vine genetics for still wine production, poor cooperage choices, and the simple fact that making a still red or white wine is just a totally different from producing vins clairs. I am in the process of writing a bit about it, as context for a vertical I did with Francis Egly back to 1975 (made by his father). In any case, we are just at the beginning. I begin to see people planting interesting massale selections from the Côte de Nuits with a view to still wine production and I’m excited to see where that goes.

If I recall correctly I tried these 2018s from Roederer when they were still undergoing élevage, back in 2019, and thought they were very promising, so I’m looking forward to seeing how these have turned out!

posts.name: Russell Faulkner
posts.cooked:
Did you try Timothee Stroebel’s William? He seems somewhat unique in prioritising still wines, in a way.

(Disclosure I sell one - in tiny amounts).

posts.name: Brad Baker
posts.cooked:
Did you try Timothee Stroebel’s William? He seems somewhat unique in prioritising still wines, in a way.

(Disclosure I sell one - in tiny amounts).

Russell,

What about Aurélien Lurquin? I think his still wines are better than his Champagnes. Oliver Horiot’s Rosé de Riceys and Coteaux Champenois rival his sparkling wines IMO too.

posts.name: Russell Faulkner
posts.cooked:
Added to the list Brad!

I hope we can connect in Champagne by the end of the year!

posts.name: William Kelley
posts.cooked:
What about Aurélien Lurquin? I think his still wines are better than his Champagnes.

Did you taste his most recent disgorgments, Brad? There was a big step up, for me!

posts.name: Brad Baker
posts.cooked:
Did you taste his most recent disgorgments, Brad? There was a big step up, for me!

I haven’t tasted anything from Lurquin since late 2019 (last time I was in Champagne) and it was 2017 material. Everything he is doing is fairly recent so you could say all is a learning, work-in-progress with big upside, but his still wines have always been not only good, but very, very good in the context of the appelation and fairly consistent. The Champagnes have gotten better from when he started, but have been quite variable from vintage to vintage. Surprisingly, I thought he knocked 2015 out of the park with his Champagnes. It wouldn’t surprise me if he gets his Champagnes at a level above his still wines, but I always felt like he had a gift for still wines.

Another producer who I really like for still wines is Hebrart. His Coteaux Champenois Blanc from Le Leon is really, really good.

My favorite still wines from Champagne are Clicquot’s, but they aren’t for sale anymore. Clos Colin is the best still red in Champagne IMO and the other still wines they make (both red and white) are amazing too. Things may change now that Dominique is gone and who knows if commercialization will ever come about, but the wines are amazing and the reds have always been amazing. Heck, Clicquot makes a dynamite in-house Ratafia too.

posts.name: Stas Medvedev
posts.cooked:
Another producer who I really like for still wines is Hebrart. His Coteaux Champenois Blanc from Le Leon is really, really good.

My favorite still wines from Champagne are Clicquot’s, but they aren’t for sale anymore. Clos Colin is the best still red in Champagne IMO and the other still wines they make (both red and white) are amazing too. Things may change now that Dominique is gone and who knows if commercialization will ever come about, but the wines are amazing and the reds have always been amazing. Heck, Clicquot makes a dynamite in-house Ratafia too.

I can only echo you with Hebrart, great Coteaux Champenois Blanc. And yes, Clicquot’s are amazing! They don’t market it as they use all of it for their Rose. Their red comes from the terroir of Bouzy. There are a few producers who make great Coteaux Champenois Rouge. I would definitely put Champagne Brice on the watch list as Cristophe Constant (ex-Paul Bara, JL Vergnon) joined them as chef de caves in 2019. They have just released a new singe vineyards bottling from the lieu-dit Le Poteau. That is Coteaux Champenois Blanc. Chardonnay from Bouzy. Limited series in Magnums only, if I am not mistaken. 60-years-old vines. Vinified in 350L barrels.

Egly-Ouriet is my all time favourite Coteaux Champenois Rouge. I also like Bereche, Geoffroy. David Leclapart is great, but that is ‘love it or hate it’ type of wine.
Drappier have been producing both red and white Coteaux Champenois for years, but to my taste these wines are very simple. They don’t even vintage them, they are sold as NV.

And speaking of still wines from Champagne we should also remind ourselves of fantastic Rosé des Riceys. This appellation where rose Pinot Noir is produced. Olivier Horiot is on top of my mind to try the wines from.

posts.name: Brad Baker
posts.cooked:
Stas,

Of the regular and public released Coteaux Champenois, Egly’s is probably my second favorite and has a clear track record. Jerome Coessens makes the best I have had recently though he hasn’t been doing it all that long. Both the Egrappée (de-stemmed) and Entière (whole cluster) are amazing wines and showcase what the warmer climate in the Côte des Bars can do. I also like what Laherte is doing with their still wines. As you mentioned, Geoffroy is another good one. Brice has had a decent still red in the past, but, yes, Christophe Constant is taking everything up a level and bringing in a tasty blanc. Everything Brice is worth paying attention to.

Through sometime in the early 1990s (???), you could actually still buy Clicquot’s Bouzy Rouge (often pure Clos Colin), but since then it has been an in-house only wine. For Clicquot, I like that for decades they have specifically tried to make still red wines and made them differently from their reds meant for the Rosés. They have always had barrels from different Bouzy plots and also from Aÿ and Verzenay as well. There have also been Meunier examples. In recent years, they also began to focus on making still whites and the early samples made by Dominique and team were phenomenal. In general, I believe you need to find the right plots and focus on making a still wine and not just choosing the best barrels of wines meant to be Champagne. Bottling some of the red you made for your Rosé or your best barrel of Chardonnay might be good, but is rarely great.

As to the Roederer wines, I like them. Earlier, unreleased vintages were also very good. A lot of thought went into the plots chosen, the harvesting, and the winemaking.
